topics.title: Promises & the Microtask Queue
topics.summary: A Promise is a placeholder for a future value. Understanding microtasks vs macrotasks, chaining, and combinators is core senior knowledge.
topics.explanation: A Promise represents the eventual result of an async operation and is always in one of three states: pending, fulfilled, or rejected. Key points seniors must know: 1) State transitions are one-way and permanent — once settled, a promise never changes. 2) Callbacks registered with .then/.catch/.finally run as MICROTASKS, not macrotasks. The microtask queue is fully drained after each macrotask (and after the current synchronous stack) BEFORE the next macrotask (setTimeout, I/O, rendering). So Promise.resolve().then(fn) runs before setTimeout(fn, 0). 3) Chaining: each .then returns a NEW promise; returning a value passes it down, returning a promise waits for it, throwing rejects the chain. 4) Error propagation: a single .catch at the end handles rejections from any earlier link. A missing .catch causes an 'unhandled rejection'. 5) Combinators — Promise.all (fail-fast, rejects on first rejection), Promise.allSettled (waits for all, never rejects), Promise.race (settles with first to settle), Promise.any (resolves with first fulfilled, rejects only if all reject with AggregateError). 6) async/await is syntactic sugar over promises — await pauses the function and schedules the continuation as a microtask. 7) Common trap: forgetting to await inside a loop, or using forEach with async (it does not wait).
topics.analogy: Promise callbacks are VIP guests (microtasks) always served before regular guests (macrotasks like setTimeout). Combinators set the party rules: all = cancel if anyone fails to arrive; allSettled = wait for everyone regardless.
keyPoints: States settle once: pending → fulfilled/rejected, permanently.
.then/.catch/.finally run as microtasks (before setTimeout).
Each .then returns a new promise; return values/promises to chain.
Combinators: all (fail-fast), allSettled (all outcomes), race, any.
await schedules its continuation as a microtask; forEach won't await.

situations.question: Your dashboard fires 5 independent API calls but renders slowly. The code awaits each call in sequence. How do you fix it, and when would sequential actually be correct?
situations.answer: The sequential awaits serialize independent requests, so total time = sum of all latencies. Fix by firing them concurrently with Promise.all (or allSettled if you want partial results when one fails): `const [a,b,c,d,e] = await Promise.all([...])`. This makes total time ≈ the slowest single request. Sequential IS correct when calls are dependent — e.g. call B needs the user ID returned by call A — or when you must avoid hammering a rate-limited endpoint, in which case you'd batch or use a concurrency limiter (p-limit) rather than full parallelism.

situations.question: A teammate uses array.forEach(async item => await save(item)) and is surprised the code after it runs before all saves finish. What's happening?
situations.answer: forEach ignores the returned promises — it does not await anything, so it kicks off all the async callbacks and returns immediately. The surrounding function continues before any save resolves. Use `for...of` with await for sequential execution, or `await Promise.all(items.map(item => save(item)))` for parallel execution where you actually wait for completion.

topics.title: Race Conditions in the Frontend
topics.summary: When async results arrive out of order or stale responses overwrite fresh state. The #1 source of subtle UI bugs.
topics.explanation: A race condition occurs when the correctness of your code depends on the timing/ordering of async operations you do not control. In frontends the classic case is the 'stale response' / out-of-order fetch: the user types 'a' then 'ab'; you fire a request per keystroke; the response for 'a' arrives AFTER 'ab' and overwrites the correct results. Senior-level mitigation patterns: 1) Cancellation — use AbortController to cancel the previous request when a new one starts (the gold standard with fetch). 2) Request sequencing — tag each request with an incrementing id and ignore any response whose id is not the latest. 3) Debounce/throttle the trigger so fewer requests fire. 4) In React, the useEffect cleanup function is the canonical place to set an 'ignore' flag or call abort. 5) For mutations, guard against double-submit (disable button, idempotency keys). Other race types: concurrent state updates (use functional setState updaters), check-then-act on shared resources, and effect re-runs. The mental model: never trust that async results arrive in the order you requested them.
topics.analogy: An out-of-order fetch race is like mailing two letters and getting replies back swapped: the reply to 'a' lands after 'ab' and overwrites the right answer. Cancel the stale one, or ignore all but the latest.
keyPoints: Bugs from async results arriving in an order you don't control.
Classic case: per-keystroke search where a stale response overwrites fresh.
Fix with AbortController — cancel the previous request.
Or tag requests with a monotonic id and drop non-latest responses.
In React, abort/ignore inside the useEffect cleanup.

situations.question: An autocomplete sometimes shows results for a previously typed query. Walk me through the root cause and the fix.
situations.answer: Root cause is an out-of-order async race: each keystroke fires a request, and responses are not guaranteed to return in the order sent — a slow response for an earlier query can resolve after the latest one and overwrite the correct results. Fix: cancel the in-flight request whenever a new one starts using AbortController (in React, abort in the useEffect cleanup). As defense-in-depth, debounce the input to reduce request volume and/or tag requests with a monotonic id and drop any response that isn't the latest. I'd also add a loading state keyed to the current query so the UI never displays mismatched data.

topics.title: Web Workers & Off-Main-Thread Work
topics.summary: Run CPU-heavy JS on a background thread to keep the UI responsive. Communicate via postMessage; no DOM access.
topics.explanation: JavaScript on a web page runs single-threaded on the 'main thread', which also handles layout, painting, and user input. Any long synchronous computation (parsing a large file, image processing, heavy data transforms) blocks rendering and freezes the UI. Web Workers let you offload that work to a separate OS thread. Key facts: 1) Workers have NO access to the DOM, window, or document — they get their own global scope (self) with fetch, WebSocket, IndexedDB, etc. 2) Communication is message-passing via postMessage / onmessage; data is structured-cloned (copied) by default. 3) For large buffers you can TRANSFER ownership (Transferable objects like ArrayBuffer) to avoid the copy cost — the sender loses access. 4) Types: Dedicated Workers (one page), Shared Workers (shared across tabs/same origin), and Service Workers (network proxy — different purpose). 5) Use SharedArrayBuffer + Atomics for true shared memory (requires cross-origin isolation headers). When to reach for a worker: anything that would block the main thread for more than ~50ms — parsing, crypto, compression, ML inference (TensorFlow.js), spreadsheet/grid calculations. When NOT to: simple async I/O (that's already non-blocking via the event loop) — workers are for CPU-bound work, not I/O-bound work.
topics.analogy: A Web Worker is a back-office assistant at a separate desk (thread): hand them heavy paperwork via notes (postMessage) so the front desk (UI) never freezes — but they can't touch the storefront (DOM).
keyPoints: Run CPU-heavy JS on a background thread; no DOM access.
Communicate via postMessage (structured clone).
Transfer ArrayBuffers to avoid the copy cost.
Use for >50ms CPU work (parsing, crypto, ML) — not I/O.
Types: Dedicated, Shared, and Service Workers.

situations.question: Users report the page freezes for a few seconds when they upload and process a large CSV. How would you diagnose and fix it?
situations.answer: Diagnose with the Performance panel — a multi-second 'Long Task' on the main thread during parse confirms CPU blocking, not network. The fix is to move the parse + transform into a Web Worker so the main thread stays free to render and respond to input. I'd post the file (or its ArrayBuffer, transferred to avoid a copy) to the worker, parse there (e.g. a streaming CSV parser), post back results or progress updates, and show a progress indicator on the main thread. If results are huge, transfer the buffer back rather than structured-cloning. This keeps the UI at 60fps while the heavy work runs in parallel.

topics.title: Worklets (Paint, Audio, Animation)
topics.summary: Lightweight, highly-restricted scripts that run inside the browser's rendering/audio pipeline for high-performance, jank-free effects.
topics.explanation: Worklets are a newer, more specialized cousin of Web Workers. They run small pieces of code at very specific points in the browser's pipeline, with extreme restrictions for performance and thread-safety. Unlike Web Workers (general-purpose background threads), worklets are purpose-built: 1) Paint Worklet (CSS Houdini) — lets you draw custom backgrounds/borders via a canvas-like API registered as a CSS paint() value, recomputed by the rendering engine as needed. 2) Animation Worklet — runs animations off the main thread, synced to the compositor and even to scroll position, so animations stay smooth even when the main thread is busy. 3) Audio Worklet — replaces the deprecated ScriptProcessorNode; runs custom audio DSP on the real-time audio rendering thread for glitch-free, low-latency audio. Key properties: worklets load tiny modules via addModule(), have no DOM access, very limited globals, and are designed to be invoked many times per frame, so the code must be fast and stateless-ish. Practical relevance for senior frontend: you rarely write paint/animation worklets day-to-day, but knowing they exist (and that Audio Worklets are the correct tool for real-time audio in the Web Audio API) signals depth. The general principle they illustrate: pushing work off the main thread and into the browser's specialized pipelines is how you achieve buttery 60fps under load.
topics.analogy: Worklets are tiny specialists embedded in the browser's pipeline: a Paint worklet draws a background, an Audio worklet processes sound in real time — small, restricted, and run many times per frame.
keyPoints: Lightweight scripts that run inside the rendering/audio pipeline.
Paint, Animation, and Audio worklets each target a pipeline stage.
No DOM access; must be fast and near-stateless.
Audio Worklet is the right tool for real-time Web Audio DSP.
They illustrate pushing work off the main thread for 60fps.

situations.question: What's the difference between a Web Worker and a Worklet, and when would you use an Audio Worklet specifically?
situations.answer: A Web Worker is a general-purpose background thread for arbitrary CPU-bound JS, communicating via postMessage. A Worklet is a tiny, highly-restricted module that the browser invokes at specific points in its rendering or audio pipeline (paint, animation frame, audio buffer) — optimized to run many times per frame with minimal overhead and no DOM access. You'd use an Audio Worklet when you need real-time, low-latency custom audio processing in the Web Audio API — for example a custom synth, effect, or visualizer — because it runs on the dedicated audio rendering thread and avoids the glitches and main-thread coupling of the old ScriptProcessorNode.

topics.title: WebSockets vs Polling vs SSE
topics.summary: Choosing how clients receive updates: short polling, long polling, Server-Sent Events, or full-duplex WebSockets.
topics.explanation: There are four common strategies for getting server updates to the client, and seniors should know the trade-offs cold. 1) Short polling — the client requests on a fixed interval (every N seconds). Simple, stateless, works everywhere, but wastes requests and adds latency up to the interval. Good for low-frequency, non-critical data. 2) Long polling — the client sends a request and the server HOLDS it open until data is available (or a timeout), then the client immediately re-requests. Near-real-time over plain HTTP, but ties up a connection per client and is awkward at scale. 3) Server-Sent Events (SSE) — a single long-lived HTTP connection over which the SERVER pushes text events (EventSource API). One-directional (server→client), auto-reconnects, works over HTTP/2, simpler than WebSockets. Great for feeds, notifications, live scores, and streaming LLM tokens. 4) WebSockets — a single TCP connection upgraded from HTTP that is FULL-DUPLEX (both sides send anytime) and low-overhead per message. Ideal for chat, collaborative editing, multiplayer, live trading. Costs: stateful connections (harder to load-balance/scale, need sticky sessions or a pub/sub backplane like Redis), must handle reconnection/backoff, heartbeats/ping-pong to detect dead connections, and auth on connect. Decision rule: server→client only and you're fine with HTTP → SSE; bidirectional/low-latency → WebSocket; trivial/infrequent → polling.
topics.analogy: Choosing a real-time transport is choosing how you get updates: keep asking 'anything new?' (polling), keep a one-way news ticker open (SSE), or hold a two-way phone call (WebSocket).
keyPoints: Short polling: simple but wasteful; latency up to the interval.
Long polling: near-real-time over HTTP, ties up connections.
SSE: one-way server→client stream, auto-reconnect, HTTP-friendly.
WebSocket: full-duplex, low overhead — chat, collaboration, trading.
Stateful sockets need sticky sessions / a pub-sub backplane to scale.

situations.question: You're building a live chat and a stock-ticker feed. Would you use the same transport for both? Justify your choices.
situations.answer: Not necessarily. Chat is bidirectional and latency-sensitive — both parties send messages, typing indicators, read receipts — so a WebSocket is the right fit (full-duplex, low per-message overhead). A stock ticker is purely server→client streaming; Server-Sent Events fit better because they're simpler, run over standard HTTP/2, auto-reconnect, and don't need the operational complexity of WebSocket scaling. Using SSE for the ticker also frees you from sticky-session/load-balancer concerns there. If the org wants one transport to simplify infra, a WebSocket can do both, but I'd weigh that against SSE's lower operational cost for the one-way feed. Either way I'd add reconnect-with-backoff and heartbeats, and scale stateful connections with a Redis (or similar) pub/sub backplane.

topics.title: Webhooks
topics.summary: Server-to-server 'reverse API': instead of you polling a provider, the provider POSTs to your endpoint when an event happens.
topics.explanation: A webhook is a user-defined HTTP callback. Instead of repeatedly asking a third party 'did anything change?' (polling), you register a URL and the provider sends an HTTP POST to it whenever an event occurs (payment succeeded, PR merged, message received). It's push-based integration between servers. Senior considerations: 1) Security — anyone could POST to your public URL, so you MUST verify authenticity. Standard approach: the provider signs the payload with a shared secret (HMAC-SHA256) and sends the signature in a header; you recompute it over the raw body and compare in constant time. Reject if it doesn't match. 2) Idempotency — networks retry, so the same event may arrive multiple times. Store processed event ids and ignore duplicates so you don't charge a card twice. 3) Respond fast (2xx) immediately, then process asynchronously (enqueue a job). Providers time out and retry if you're slow, causing duplicate deliveries. 4) Ordering is NOT guaranteed — design handlers to tolerate out-of-order events (use the resource's current state, not the event sequence). 5) Retries & dead-letter — providers retry failed deliveries with backoff; have a way to replay missed events. 6) Local testing — use tunneling tools (ngrok) since the provider needs a public URL. Note: webhooks are server-side; a pure SPA can't receive them directly — you need a backend endpoint (often a serverless function) to receive and then notify the client via WebSocket/SSE/push.
topics.analogy: A webhook is the provider calling YOU when something happens, instead of you repeatedly calling to ask: register a URL and they POST events to it — but verify it's really them and expect duplicates.
keyPoints: Push-based: the provider POSTs events to your URL.
Verify authenticity with an HMAC signature over the raw body.
Make processing idempotent — the same event can arrive twice.
ACK fast (2xx), then process asynchronously.
Ordering isn't guaranteed; base logic on current state.

situations.question: Your payment provider's webhook occasionally fires twice for the same charge, and a customer got double-credited. How do you prevent this?
situations.answer: This is the idempotency problem — providers retry deliveries (e.g. when your endpoint was slow or returned a non-2xx), so the same event can arrive more than once. The fix is to make processing idempotent: every webhook event has a unique id; before acting, atomically record that id (a unique constraint / upsert in the DB) and skip if it already exists. Pair that with acknowledging the webhook with a 2xx immediately and doing the actual crediting in a background job, so slow processing doesn't trigger provider retries in the first place. I'd also verify the HMAC signature so only legitimate events are processed, and never rely on event ordering — base the credit on the charge's authoritative state.

topics.title: Web & Mobile Push Notifications
topics.summary: Delivering messages to users even when the app is closed — via the Push API + Service Worker (web) or FCM/APNs (mobile).
topics.explanation: Push notifications let a server deliver a message to a device even when your app/tab isn't open. The architecture differs by platform but shares a model: the client obtains a token/subscription from a platform push service, sends it to your backend, and your backend uses it to push messages through that service. WEB PUSH: 1) Register a Service Worker (required — it's what wakes up to receive the push when the page is closed). 2) Request Notification permission (must be user-gesture-driven; over-asking gets you blocked). 3) Call pushManager.subscribe with a VAPID public key to get a PushSubscription (endpoint + keys). 4) Send that subscription to your server. 5) Server sends an encrypted payload to the subscription endpoint (via a library like web-push); the browser's push service delivers it, the Service Worker's 'push' event fires and calls showNotification. MOBILE (React Native): you use Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) for Android and Apple Push Notification service (APNs) for iOS (FCM can proxy both). You request permission, get a device token, store it server-side, and send via FCM/APNs. Senior concerns: permission UX (ask in context, not on load), token rotation/expiry and cleanup of dead subscriptions, payload size limits, handling foreground vs background delivery differently, deep-linking on tap, and respecting OS notification settings. Privacy/regulation: notifications are opt-in and abuse leads to revocation.
topics.analogy: Push is the platform's delivery service waking your app even when it's closed: you get a delivery token, hand it to your server, and the server sends messages through the platform's push service.
keyPoints: Web: Service Worker + Push API + a VAPID subscription.
Mobile: FCM (Android) and APNs (iOS), often via FCM.
Ask permission in-context, never on page load.
Store and refresh tokens; prune dead subscriptions (410 Gone).
Handle foreground vs background and deep-link on tap.

situations.question: Conversion on your push opt-in is terrible and some users report never seeing notifications. What would you change?
situations.answer: Two separate problems. Opt-in rate: never request permission on page load with no context — browsers and users punish that, and a denial is often permanent. Instead, ask in-context right after a relevant action (e.g. 'Get notified when your order ships?') behind a soft pre-prompt you control, so a 'no' doesn't burn the native permission. Delivery: 'never seeing them' usually means the Service Worker isn't registered/active, the subscription expired or was never saved, or OS-level notification settings are off. I'd verify the SW is installed and the push event handler calls showNotification, implement subscription refresh and prune dead endpoints server-side (push services return 410 Gone), and confirm we handle foreground vs background correctly. I'd also track delivery + click metrics to distinguish 'not delivered' from 'not tapped'.

topics.title: Service Workers & PWAs
topics.summary: A programmable network proxy between your app and the network — enables offline, caching strategies, background sync, and push.
topics.explanation: A Service Worker (SW) is a script the browser runs in the background, separate from the page, that sits between your app and the network and can intercept every request (the 'fetch' event). It's the foundation of Progressive Web Apps. Key facts: 1) Lifecycle — install (cache the app shell / static assets), activate (clean up old caches), then it controls pages and intercepts fetches. Updates are tricky: a new SW installs in the background and waits until all tabs close (or you skipWaiting) before activating. 2) HTTPS only (except localhost) because it's a powerful man-in-the-middle. 3) No DOM access; it's event-driven and can be killed/restarted at any time, so keep no in-memory state — use Cache Storage / IndexedDB. 4) Caching strategies you must know: Cache-First (serve cache, fall back to network — best for static assets), Network-First (try network, fall back to cache — best for fresh-ish data), Stale-While-Revalidate (serve cache immediately, update cache in background — best UX for many APIs), Cache-Only, Network-Only. 5) It also enables Background Sync (retry failed POSTs when connectivity returns) and is REQUIRED to receive Push notifications. 6) Tooling: Workbox abstracts the boilerplate. Pitfalls: caching too aggressively serves stale code (version your caches and have an update flow), and a buggy SW can break your whole site for returning users — always ship a kill-switch / clean unregister path.
topics.analogy: A Service Worker is a programmable receptionist between your app and the network: it intercepts every request, can serve cached copies offline, and receive push — but a buggy one can trap users on stale files.
keyPoints: A network proxy script that intercepts fetch — the basis of PWAs.
Lifecycle: install (precache) → activate (cleanup) → control.
HTTPS only; no DOM; keep no in-memory state.
Strategies: cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate.
Version caches and ship a kill-switch to avoid stale-app lockout.

situations.question: After deploying a Service Worker, some users keep seeing an old broken version of the app even after you ship fixes. What went wrong and how do you recover?
situations.answer: Classic SW staleness: the worker cached the app shell (HTML/JS) and is serving it cache-first, so returning users get the old assets, and the new SW only activates after all tabs of the app are closed — which rarely happens. Recovery: ship a corrected SW that versions its caches (new cache name), calls skipWaiting() + clients.claim() so it takes over immediately, and deletes old caches on activate. Notify the page when a new SW is waiting and prompt a reload. For the truly broken case, deploy a 'reset' SW that unregisters itself and clears caches so clients fall back to the network. Going forward I'd never cache the HTML cache-first (use network-first or stale-while-revalidate for navigation), version every cache, and keep an update/kill-switch flow.

topics.title: Authentication & Authorization (Sessions, JWT, OAuth)
topics.summary: Proving who a user is (authn) and what they can do (authz). Sessions vs tokens, JWTs, refresh flows, OAuth/OIDC.
topics.explanation: Authentication (authn) = verifying identity; Authorization (authz) = verifying permissions. Two dominant server-trust models: 1) SESSION-based (stateful) — server creates a session, stores it (DB/Redis), and sends the client an opaque session id in an httpOnly cookie. Server looks it up on each request. Easy to revoke, but requires shared session storage to scale. 2) TOKEN-based (stateless) — server issues a signed JWT (JSON Web Token: header.payload.signature, base64url). The client sends it (usually Authorization: Bearer). The server verifies the signature and trusts the claims without a lookup. Scales well, but JWTs can't be easily revoked before expiry, so you keep access tokens SHORT-lived (5–15 min) and pair them with a long-lived REFRESH token to get new ones. Critical frontend security: 3) Storing tokens — localStorage is readable by any XSS-injected script (token theft); httpOnly Secure SameSite cookies are not readable by JS and are the safer default, but cookies need CSRF protection (SameSite=Lax/Strict + CSRF tokens for state-changing requests). 4) JWTs are signed, not encrypted — never put secrets in the payload; anyone can decode it. 5) OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect — delegated auth ('Login with Google'). Use the Authorization Code flow with PKCE for SPAs/mobile (never the implicit flow anymore). 6) Authorization should be enforced on the SERVER — hiding a button in the UI is not security. Frontend role checks are UX only.
topics.analogy: Auth is building security: authentication checks your ID at the door (who are you?), authorization is which floors your badge opens (what can you do?). Frontend checks are courtesy signs; the real locks are on the server.
keyPoints: Sessions (stateful, easy to revoke) vs JWTs (stateless, scale well).
Keep access tokens short-lived; refresh with a long-lived token.
localStorage is XSS-exposed; httpOnly cookies need CSRF defense.
JWTs are signed, not encrypted — never put secrets in them.
For SPAs/mobile use OAuth Authorization Code + PKCE.

situations.question: A teammate wants to store the JWT in localStorage 'because it's simple and survives refresh.' What are the risks and what do you recommend?
situations.answer: localStorage is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page, so a single XSS vulnerability lets an attacker exfiltrate the token and fully impersonate the user — and you can't revoke a stateless JWT before it expires. I'd recommend storing the access token in memory (lost on refresh, which is fine) and keeping a long-lived refresh token in an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie that JS can't read; on load or 401 you silently refresh. Cookies reintroduce CSRF risk, so I'd use SameSite=Lax/Strict plus CSRF tokens on state-changing requests. Keep access tokens short-lived (5–15 min) to limit the blast radius, and enforce all real authorization on the server — the token storage choice is about limiting theft, not a substitute for server-side checks.

situations.question: What's the difference between authentication and authorization, and why is doing role checks only on the frontend dangerous?
situations.answer: Authentication answers 'who are you?' (verifying identity via credentials/tokens); authorization answers 'what are you allowed to do?' (permissions/roles). Frontend role checks — hiding an admin button or route — are purely UX: they prevent confusion, not access. A malicious user can call the API directly with their valid token, bypassing the UI entirely. So every privileged action must be authorized on the server, which validates the token and checks the user's actual permissions before performing the operation. The frontend check is a convenience layer on top of server-enforced authorization, never the enforcement itself.

topics.title: API Key Management & Secrets
topics.summary: API keys identify/authorize an app to a service. The cardinal rule: never ship secret keys in frontend code.
topics.explanation: An API key is a token that identifies and authorizes a calling application to an API. The single most important senior principle: anything shipped to the browser or a mobile bundle is PUBLIC. View-source, devtools network tab, and decompiled APKs all expose strings in your client code. Therefore: 1) SECRET keys (that can spend money, read private data, or act with broad privilege) must NEVER be in frontend code, env vars baked into the bundle, or mobile binaries. They live on a server/proxy; the client calls YOUR backend, which holds the secret and calls the third party. 2) PUBLISHABLE/restricted keys (e.g. Stripe publishable key, Google Maps browser key, Firebase config) are designed to be public BUT must be locked down with referrer/origin/IP restrictions, scopes, and quotas so a leaked key can't be abused. 3) Mobile: there is no truly secure place to store a secret in a shipped app; treat embedded keys as compromised and rely on server-side checks, key restriction, and short-lived tokens minted by your backend. 4) Operational hygiene — never commit keys to git (use secret managers / CI secrets / .env that's gitignored), rotate keys regularly and immediately on suspected leak, scope keys to least privilege, monitor usage for anomalies, and add rate limiting. 5) For third-party APIs that must be called from the client, build a thin backend proxy that injects the secret, enforces auth, and rate-limits. The common interview trap is wanting to call OpenAI/Stripe/etc. directly from React — the answer is always a backend proxy.
topics.analogy: Anything shipped to the browser is printed on the outside of the box — view-source and devtools read it. Secret keys must live on a server; the client calls YOUR backend, which holds the secret.
keyPoints: Anything in the client bundle/app is PUBLIC.
Secret keys live server-side; the client calls your backend proxy.
Publishable keys can be client-side but must be origin/scope-locked.
Mobile binaries can't hide secrets — restrict + use short-lived tokens.
Never commit keys; rotate on leak; rate-limit the proxy.

situations.question: A junior added the company's third-party AI API key to a React .env file and the app works fine in the demo. Why is this a serious problem?
situations.answer: Vite/CRA-style env vars prefixed for the client are inlined into the JavaScript bundle at build time — the key is plainly visible in the shipped assets and the network tab, so it's effectively published to the world. Anyone can extract it and run up unlimited charges or access data under your account, and it's likely also committed to git history. The fix: revoke and rotate that key immediately, move all calls to that API behind a backend endpoint that holds the secret in server-side env/secret-manager, and have the frontend authenticate to our own backend instead. Add per-user rate limiting and usage monitoring on the proxy. Only ever put publishable/restricted keys in the client, and even then lock them by origin and scope.

topics.title: SSL / Certificate Pinning
topics.summary: Hard-coding the expected server certificate (or its public key) in the app to defeat man-in-the-middle attacks, even with a trusted-but-rogue CA.
topics.explanation: Normal TLS trusts any certificate signed by a CA in the device's trust store. That's usually fine, but it breaks down when an attacker can get a fraudulent-but-valid certificate — a compromised/rogue CA, a corporate or malware-installed root CA, or a user who installs a proxy cert to intercept traffic. SSL/Certificate Pinning hardens this by making the app trust ONLY a specific certificate or public key that you pin (embed) in the app, rejecting all others even if they're 'valid' per the system trust store. This is primarily a MOBILE concern (React Native / native apps), where it meaningfully raises the bar against MITM and against users sniffing the app's API traffic. Forms: 1) Certificate pinning — pin the exact cert; breaks when the cert is renewed (must ship an app update). 2) Public-key (SPKI) pinning — pin the public key; survives cert renewal as long as the key is reused. More flexible; the common recommendation. Best practices: pin a BACKUP key too so a key rotation doesn't brick every installed app, set sane expiry, and have a remote kill-switch/rollout plan. Implementation in RN: libraries like react-native-ssl-pinning or TrustKit / OkHttp CertificatePinner (Android) and configuration on iOS. Caveats: pinning is NOT relevant to standard web apps (the browser manages TLS and you can't pin from JS in a normal site — HPKP was deprecated). It also complicates legitimate debugging (your own proxy like Charles will be blocked) and operations (cert rotation needs coordination). It's a defense-in-depth measure, not a replacement for HTTPS — and a determined attacker who can re-sign the app can strip it, so combine it with other protections.
topics.analogy: SSL pinning is recognizing a friend's actual face, not just any ID badge: the app trusts only the certificate/key you embedded, rejecting even 'valid' CA-signed certs from a man-in-the-middle.
keyPoints: Trust only a pinned cert/public key, defeating rogue-CA MITM.
Primarily a mobile concern (not standard web).
Public-key (SPKI) pinning survives cert renewal; cert pinning doesn't.
Always pin a backup key so rotation doesn't brick installed apps.
Defense-in-depth on top of TLS, not a replacement.

situations.question: Security asks you to add SSL pinning to the mobile app. What do you implement, what could break, and how do you avoid bricking users?
situations.answer: I'd implement public-key (SPKI) pinning rather than pinning the exact certificate, so routine certificate renewals don't break the app as long as the key is reused — using OkHttp's CertificatePinner on Android and TrustKit/NSURLSession config (or a library like react-native-ssl-pinning) cross-platform. The big risk is bricking: if we pin a single key and the server rotates to a new key, every installed app instantly fails to connect and the only fix is an app update users may not install. To avoid that I pin at least one BACKUP key that's pre-provisioned server-side, coordinate cert/key rotation with the backend team, and keep a remote config/kill-switch to disable pinning if something goes wrong. I'd also document that pinning will block our own debugging proxies, and treat it as defense-in-depth on top of TLS, not a standalone guarantee.

topics.title: Redux Thunk & Async Middleware
topics.summary: Redux is synchronous; thunks are the middleware that lets action creators return functions for side effects like API calls.
topics.explanation: Vanilla Redux dispatch only accepts plain action objects and the reducer must be a pure synchronous function — so where do API calls and other async side effects go? Middleware. Redux Thunk is the minimal answer: it lets an action creator return a FUNCTION (a 'thunk') instead of an action object. The thunk receives (dispatch, getState) and can run async logic, read current state, and dispatch multiple actions over time. The canonical pattern is the three-action async lifecycle: dispatch a 'pending/request' action (set loading), await the API, then dispatch 'fulfilled/success' with the data OR 'rejected/failure' with the error. Reducers handle each to update loading/data/error state. Key points: 1) Thunks keep components dumb — the component dispatches one thunk, not orchestrating fetch + multiple dispatches. 2) getState lets you guard (e.g. skip the fetch if data is already cached/fresh). 3) Thunks compose — a thunk can dispatch other thunks. 4) Modern Redux Toolkit (RTK) builds on this: createAsyncThunk auto-generates the pending/fulfilled/rejected action types and handles dispatching them, drastically cutting boilerplate; RTK Query goes further and removes most data-fetching code entirely (caching, dedup, invalidation). 5) Thunk vs Saga: thunks are simple and imperative; redux-saga uses generators for complex async flows (cancellation, debouncing, racing, long-running watchers) at the cost of a steeper learning curve. For most apps, thunks (via RTK) are the right default; reach for sagas only when you have genuinely complex orchestration. Seniors should know the lifecycle, error/loading handling, and that RTK is the modern way.
topics.analogy: Redux is synchronous by nature; a thunk is a permission slip that lets an action be a FUNCTION instead of a plain object — so it can do async work (fetch) and dispatch several actions over time.
keyPoints: Middleware that lets action creators return functions.
A thunk receives (dispatch, getState) for async logic.
Classic pattern: dispatch pending → fulfilled/rejected.
RTK's createAsyncThunk auto-generates that lifecycle.
Thunks for side effects; RTK Query for server cache; sagas for complex orchestration.

situations.question: When would you choose Redux Thunk, Redux Saga, or RTK Query for handling async data, and why?
situations.answer: RTK Query is my default for server data — it eliminates almost all hand-written fetching/caching/loading code, dedupes requests, and handles cache invalidation, which is what most 'async Redux' actually is. Plain thunks (via createAsyncThunk) are the right choice for one-off side effects or imperative async logic that isn't a cache-able query — simple, readable, easy to test. Redux Saga earns its complexity only when you have genuinely complex orchestration: cancellation, debouncing/throttling at the effect level, racing concurrent tasks, or long-running 'watcher' flows like websocket handling — its generator model expresses those cleanly where thunks get messy. So: server cache → RTK Query; simple side effects → thunk; complex concurrent workflows → saga. I'd avoid pulling in saga's learning curve unless the orchestration genuinely needs it.

topics.title: Core Web Vitals & Performance Budgets
topics.summary: Google's user-centric metrics — LCP, INP, CLS — and how to measure, debug, and enforce performance budgets.
topics.explanation: Core Web Vitals are the metrics Google uses to measure real-user page experience, and they directly affect SEO rankings. Every senior frontend engineer must know them cold. 1) LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) — time until the largest image/text block is painted. Target: <2.5s. Main culprits: unoptimized hero images, render-blocking resources, slow server response. Fixes: preload the hero image (<link rel=preload>), serve modern formats (WebP/AVIF), use a CDN, defer non-critical JS. 2) INP (Interaction to Next Paint, replaced FID in 2024) — worst-case latency from a user interaction to the next paint. Target: <200ms. Main culprits: long JS tasks blocking the main thread, heavy event handlers. Fixes: break long tasks with scheduler.yield() / setTimeout(0), move CPU work to workers, avoid layout thrash. 3) CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) — sum of unexpected layout shifts. Target: <0.1. Main culprits: images/iframes without size attributes, dynamically injected content above existing content, web font FOUT. Fixes: always set explicit width/height on media, reserve space for ads/embeds, use font-display: optional. Tooling: measure with Lighthouse (lab), PageSpeed Insights (field/CrUX), Web Vitals JS library, and Chrome UX Report. Performance budgets codify acceptable thresholds and can be enforced in CI (Lighthouse CI, bundlesize). The key mindset shift: optimize for the 75th percentile of field data, not just your fast dev machine.
topics.analogy: Core Web Vitals are a health checkup for your page: LCP = how fast the main content shows, CLS = how much things jump around, INP = how snappy it feels when tapped.
keyPoints: LCP <2.5s: preload hero image, WebP/AVIF, fast TTFB.
INP <200ms: break long JS tasks, offload to workers.
CLS <0.1: size images, reserve space, control font swap.
Optimize for field data (CrUX/PageSpeed), not your fast machine.
Enforce budgets in CI (Lighthouse CI).

situations.question: A product manager reports that the marketing page dropped 20 positions in Google search after a new hero video was added. How do you diagnose and fix it?
situations.answer: The hero video almost certainly tanked LCP and possibly CLS. I'd run PageSpeed Insights on the page to see field data and identify which metric dropped, then use the Lighthouse filmstrip and Coverage panel to confirm the video is delaying LCP. Fixes: replace the video with a poster image for the initial paint (use the <video poster> attribute and lazy-load the actual video with preload=none), serve a WebP/AVIF static hero that paints immediately, and set explicit dimensions on the container to prevent layout shift when the video loads. If the video must play on load, preload only the first frame, stream via a CDN, and ensure it has explicit width/height. I'd verify improvements with Lighthouse CI and check CrUX data over 28 days to confirm field scores recover.

topics.title: Code Splitting & Lazy Loading
topics.summary: Shipping only the JS a user needs right now — dynamic imports, React.lazy, route-based splitting, prefetching.
topics.explanation: The single biggest performance win for large SPAs is not sending code the user hasn't asked for yet. Code splitting lets you break your bundle into chunks and load them on demand. Key techniques: 1) Dynamic import() — any import() call is automatically a split point in Webpack/Vite, producing a separate chunk loaded on demand. This is the primitive everything else builds on. 2) React.lazy + Suspense — wraps a dynamic import to render a component that loads its chunk on first render, with a Suspense fallback for loading state. Best used at route level. 3) Route-based splitting — the most impactful level: each route gets its own chunk, so a user paying a bill doesn't download the admin dashboard. With React Router, wrap each lazy route in Suspense. 4) Library splitting — large libs (moment, lodash, chart libraries) that are only used in one feature should be in that feature's chunk, not the main bundle. 5) Prefetching/preloading — after the current page loads, speculatively load the next likely chunk (modulepreload or webpack magic comments: /* webpackPrefetch: true */). This removes the latency of on-demand loading for predictable navigations. 6) Tree shaking — eliminating dead code at build time (complementary to splitting). 7) Analyze the bundle with Webpack Bundle Analyzer or Vite's rollup-plugin-visualizer to find what's big and where. Senior signal: knowing that lazy loading without a thoughtful fallback UX degrades perceived performance — the user sees a blank/spinner on navigation. Preload on hover and skeleton screens address this.
topics.analogy: Code splitting is packing one bag per activity instead of one giant suitcase: users download only the screen they're on, and the rest loads on demand.
keyPoints: Break the bundle into chunks loaded on demand via import().
React.lazy + Suspense splits at the component level.
Route-based splitting is the highest-impact move.
Split heavy, infrequent libraries into their own chunks.
Prefetch the next likely chunk on hover/idle.

situations.question: Your SPA's initial bundle is 2MB and the first load takes 8 seconds on a typical mobile connection. Walk me through your approach to fixing it.
situations.answer: Start by analyzing the bundle with vite-bundle-visualizer or webpack-bundle-analyzer to see what's actually large — often 60% is one or two libraries. Then: 1) Route-split every page behind React.lazy so users only load the current route's code. 2) Move large infrequent libraries (charts, rich text editors, PDF renderers) to their own dynamic chunk. 3) Replace lodash/moment with tree-shakeable alternatives or native equivalents. 4) Enable gzip/brotli compression on the server. 5) Split vendor chunks so browser-cacheable common deps don't re-download on every release. 6) Prefetch the next likely route chunk on hover to hide async-load latency. 7) Preload the LCP image and defer render-blocking scripts. Target: main bundle <150KB gzipped, with route chunks loaded on demand, validated with Lighthouse CI.

topics.title: Monorepos & Build Systems (Turborepo/Nx)
topics.summary: Managing multiple related packages in one repo — shared tooling, incremental builds, task orchestration at scale.
topics.explanation: A monorepo is a single git repository containing multiple distinct packages or applications. As frontend teams grow past one app, a monorepo solves several pain points: shared component libraries, shared utilities, consistent tooling and lint rules, atomic cross-package changes, and easier cross-project refactors. Tooling landscape: 1) Workspaces — npm/yarn/pnpm workspaces handle dependency hoisting and local package linking. The foundation. 2) Turborepo — build orchestration layer: defines tasks (build, test, lint) and the dependency graph between them, caches task outputs (locally and remotely), and runs tasks in parallel where dependencies allow. A turbo run build only rebuilds packages whose inputs changed. 3) Nx — richer alternative with first-class support for many frameworks, project graph visualization, affected commands (only run tasks for packages changed vs main), generators (code scaffolding), and module federation support. 4) Changesets — manages versioning and changelogs for publishable packages. Key concepts seniors must own: task pipeline — package B's build must run after package A's build if B depends on A; cache keys — turbo/nx hash inputs (source files, env vars, lockfile) to decide if a task output is reusable; remote caching — share build cache across CI machines and team members so you never rebuild what hasn't changed (biggest CI time win). Trade-offs: monorepos simplify sharing but require disciplined boundaries (packages shouldn't have circular deps), and repo size can slow git operations at extreme scale (Meta-scale tools like Bazel exist for that). For most product teams at 2–20 packages, Turborepo is the pragmatic entry point.
topics.analogy: A monorepo is one repo holding many packages that share tooling and can change atomically. Turborepo/Nx are the smart build managers that rebuild only what changed and cache the rest.
keyPoints: One repo, many packages, shared tooling, atomic cross-package changes.
Workspaces (pnpm/yarn) link local packages.
Turborepo/Nx run a task graph and cache outputs (local + remote).
'affected' commands run tasks only for changed packages.
Remote caching across CI is the biggest time win.

situations.question: Your team has a main app, a component library, and an internal utilities package across 3 repos and changes constantly break the app when the lib updates. How would you restructure this?
situations.answer: This is the classic multi-repo problem: out-of-sync versions, no atomic cross-package changes, and no visibility into what a lib change affects. I'd migrate to a monorepo with pnpm workspaces and Turborepo. The component lib and utils become workspace packages (packages/ui, packages/utils) referenced by the app with workspace:* — so changes are immediately visible without publishing. Turborepo's task pipeline ensures libs build before the app, and remote caching means CI only rebuilds affected packages. I'd add Changesets for versioning if any of these need to be published externally. The key benefit: a single PR can change the lib and the app together, CI runs the full affected graph, and you can't ship a breaking lib change without simultaneously updating its consumers.

topics.title: Accessibility (a11y) in Depth
topics.summary: Building UIs usable by everyone — semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard navigation, screen-reader testing, and WCAG compliance.
topics.explanation: Accessibility is not a checkbox — it's a quality dimension like performance. Senior engineers design with it from the start, not as an afterthought. The layered approach: 1) Semantic HTML first — use the right element for the job (<button> not <div onClick>, <nav>, <main>, <h1>-<h6> in order, <label for> with inputs). Browsers give you keyboard support, roles, and states for free. 2) ARIA when HTML falls short — ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes add roles, states, and properties that screen readers consume when the native semantic isn't available: role=dialog, aria-expanded, aria-label, aria-describedby, aria-live for dynamic regions. The first rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA if native HTML does the job. Incorrect ARIA is worse than no ARIA. 3) Keyboard navigation — every interactive element must be reachable and operable via keyboard: logical tab order (tabIndex=0, avoid positive tabindex), visible focus styles (don't remove outline without a replacement), and keyboard event handlers for custom widgets (Escape closes modals, arrow keys navigate menus per ARIA patterns). 4) Color and contrast — WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast for normal text, 3:1 for large text. Don't convey meaning by color alone. 5) Focus management — when opening a modal, move focus inside; on close, return focus to the trigger. 6) Testing: automated tools catch ~30-40% of issues — use axe-core (jest-axe, @axe-core/react, Lighthouse). The rest requires keyboard testing and a screen reader (NVDA/Windows, VoiceOver/Mac, TalkBack/Android). 7) WCAG 2.1 AA is the legal standard in many jurisdictions (ADA in the US, EN 301 549 in the EU).
topics.analogy: Accessibility is building ramps alongside the stairs from the start: semantic HTML and keyboard support come free, ARIA fills the gaps, and a screen-reader pass catches what automated tools (only ~40%) miss.
keyPoints: Semantic HTML first; reach for ARIA only when needed.
Every interactive element must be keyboard-operable with visible focus.
Manage focus in modals; return focus to the trigger on close.
WCAG AA contrast: 4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 large text.
Automated tools catch ~40% — test with a real screen reader.

situations.question: A custom dropdown you built works with mouse but a screen reader user reports it's unusable and can't navigate the options. What do you fix?
situations.answer: A custom dropdown needs explicit ARIA to communicate its widget role and state to screen readers. I'd add role=combobox (or button + role=listbox pattern), aria-expanded on the trigger, aria-haspopup=listbox, aria-controls pointing to the option list, and role=option on each item with aria-selected. Keyboard handling: Enter/Space to open, arrow keys to navigate options, Escape to close and return focus to the trigger, Home/End to jump to first/last — these are the ARIA Authoring Practices patterns. I'd also add a visible focus ring so keyboard-only users can see where they are. After implementing, I'd test with VoiceOver and NVDA to verify announcements are correct, and add a jest-axe test. The root lesson: whenever you replace a native <select> with a custom component, you take on all the accessibility work the browser was doing for free.

topics.title: Memory Leaks & Profiling
topics.summary: Finding and fixing JS memory leaks — retained closures, detached DOM, forgotten listeners, and heap snapshot analysis.
topics.explanation: JavaScript is garbage-collected, but GC can't collect objects that are still reachable — and a memory leak is an object you no longer need that remains reachable due to a forgotten reference. Memory leaks in SPAs cause progressively worse performance and eventual crashes in long-running sessions. Common patterns: 1) Uncleared intervals/timeouts — setInterval keeps its callback and all captured closures alive. Always clearInterval in cleanup. 2) Forgotten event listeners — addEventListener without removeEventListener. Especially dangerous on global objects (window, document) from component code. 3) Detached DOM nodes — a JS reference holds a DOM element that was removed from the tree; the whole subtree is GC-blocked. 4) Closures capturing large objects — a long-lived callback captures a large scope unnecessarily. 5) Growing caches/stores without eviction — an in-memory cache that never prunes. 6) React-specific: setState after unmount (now handled by React 18+), subscriptions/event emitters not unsubscribed in useEffect cleanup, WebSocket/SSE connections not closed. Profiling tools: Chrome Memory panel — take a heap snapshot, look for 'Detached <Element>' nodes; take two snapshots and compare to find objects allocated between them. Allocation Timeline tracks allocations over time. The Performance panel shows memory usage over time to confirm a leak pattern. Fix pattern: always clean up in useEffect return, use AbortController for fetch, clear timers, unsubscribe from observables/event emitters, and WeakMap/WeakRef for caches that should allow GC.
topics.analogy: A leak is leaving sticky notes (references) pointing at things you no longer need, so the janitor (GC) can't throw them out: forgotten timers, listeners, and subscriptions pile up until the app crashes.
keyPoints: GC can't collect what's still reachable — leaks are stray references.
Usual suspects: uncleared intervals, listeners, detached DOM, growing caches.
In React, every subscribing effect must clean up in its return.
Diagnose with DevTools heap snapshots (compare two over time).
Use AbortController/clearInterval/unsubscribe; WeakMap for GC-friendly caches.

situations.question: Users report the app becomes sluggish and eventually crashes after leaving it open for several hours. How do you investigate and fix a suspected memory leak?
situations.answer: I'd confirm the leak pattern in Chrome DevTools: open the Memory panel, take a baseline heap snapshot, use the app for 15–30 minutes, force GC, then take another snapshot. If memory keeps growing, I'd use the 'Comparison' view to find what objects are accumulating — Detached DOM nodes and growing arrays/maps are the usual suspects. Allocation Timeline is useful if I can't pinpoint from snapshots. Common fixes based on findings: ensure every setInterval/setTimeout has a corresponding clear in useEffect cleanup, remove event listeners (especially on window/document) on cleanup, close WebSocket/SSE connections on unmount, and unsubscribe from any observable/event emitter. For React I'd audit useEffect hooks — each one that creates a subscription, timer, or listener must have a cleanup return. I'd also check for caches that grow unbounded and add eviction. After fixing, I'd replay the same usage session and verify the heap stays flat.

topics.title: Micro Frontends
topics.summary: Splitting a large frontend into independently developed, deployed, and owned pieces — the frontend analog of microservices.
topics.explanation: Micro frontends (MFEs) extend the microservices idea to the browser: instead of one monolithic SPA owned by one big team, you decompose the UI into independently buildable, deployable, and team-owned applications that compose into one product. Motivation: let many teams ship autonomously, scale org-wise, mix tech/versions, and isolate failures. Composition strategies: 1) Build-time — publish each MFE as an npm package the container imports. Simple but couples deploys (you must rebuild the shell to update a part) — arguably not 'true' MFE. 2) Run-time via JavaScript — the container fetches and mounts each MFE's bundle at runtime; Webpack 5 MODULE FEDERATION is the popular enabler (apps expose/consume modules across independently deployed builds, sharing dependencies like React). 3) Run-time via iframes — strong isolation, weak integration/UX. 4) Edge/server-side composition — stitch fragments server-side (e.g. with a framework like a BFF or tools like Tailor/Podium). Hard problems seniors must address: shared dependencies and version skew (don't ship 4 copies of React — share singletons), consistent design system/UX across teams, routing between MFEs, cross-MFE communication (custom events, shared state via a thin pub/sub — avoid tight coupling), auth/session sharing, performance (bundle duplication, many network requests), and consistent observability. The honest take: MFEs solve an ORGANIZATIONAL scaling problem (many teams) at real technical cost. For a single team, a well-structured modular monolith/monorepo is usually better. Adopt MFEs when team autonomy and independent deployment outweigh the integration complexity.
topics.analogy: Micro-frontends are a food court: each team runs its own stall (app), independently deployed, sharing the seating (shell). Great for many teams, but you pay in coordination overhead.
keyPoints: Independently built/deployed, team-owned UI pieces.
Module Federation loads remotes at runtime; share React as a singleton.
Solves an ORGANIZATIONAL scaling problem, at real technical cost.
Watch version skew, design consistency, and auth/state sharing.
For a single team, a modular monorepo is usually better.

situations.question: Leadership wants to migrate a monolithic React SPA to micro frontends 'to move faster.' How do you evaluate whether that's the right call?
situations.answer: I'd push back on 'faster' as the goal and ask what's actually slow. Micro frontends solve an organizational problem — multiple teams stepping on each other in one codebase/deploy pipeline and wanting independent ownership and release cadence. If we have many teams blocked on a shared release train, MFEs (e.g. via Module Federation) genuinely help by enabling independent deploys. But they add real cost: dependency version skew, design-system consistency, cross-app communication, auth sharing, more network requests, and harder end-to-end debugging — for a single or small team that overhead usually makes you slower, not faster. So my recommendation depends on team topology: if it's org-scaling pain, pilot one MFE boundary along a clean domain seam with shared singletons and a shared design system before committing. If it's really build times or code organization within one team, a modular monorepo with good boundaries gets most of the benefit without the distributed-system tax.

topics.title: Caching Strategies (Browser → CDN → Server)
topics.summary: Storing computed/fetched results closer to the consumer to cut latency and load — across many layers, each with its own invalidation rules.
topics.explanation: Caching is everywhere in a frontend stack, and 'there are only two hard problems: cache invalidation and naming things.' Seniors should reason about the LAYERS: 1) HTTP caching — Cache-Control headers (max-age, no-cache, no-store, private/public, immutable), ETag/If-None-Match and Last-Modified/If-Modified-Since for conditional requests (304 Not Modified saves bandwidth). The pattern for static assets: long max-age + content-HASHED filenames (app.a1b2c3.js) so you cache forever yet bust automatically on change; for HTML use no-cache so users get the new asset references. 2) CDN/edge caching — cache responses geographically close to users; key concerns are cache keys, purging/invalidation on deploy, and stale-while-revalidate. 3) Service Worker / Cache Storage — programmatic offline + strategy control (cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate). 4) In-app data cache — React Query/RTK Query/SWR cache server state with staleTime, dedup, background refetch, and invalidation on mutation; this is where most 'app feels fast' wins come from. 5) Memory/computation caching — memoization (useMemo, reselect selectors, lodash.memoize) to avoid recomputing derived data. 6) Server/DB caching (Redis, Memcached) and the cache-aside pattern. Cross-cutting concepts: cache invalidation strategies (TTL/expiry, write-through, write-back, explicit purge, event-driven), the stampede/thundering-herd problem (many clients miss at once — mitigate with request coalescing, jittered TTLs, stale-while-revalidate), and cache-control of PRIVATE/personalized data (never cache authenticated responses in shared caches). The senior skill is choosing the right layer and an invalidation strategy you can actually reason about.
topics.analogy: Caching is keeping copies closer to whoever needs them — browser, CDN, app, server — to cut latency. The hard part isn't storing; it's knowing when a copy is stale (invalidation).
keyPoints: Layers: HTTP, CDN/edge, Service Worker, in-app data, memory, server.
Static assets: long max-age + content-hashed filenames; HTML no-cache.
ETag/304 avoids re-downloading unchanged data.
Invalidate via TTL, explicit purge, or events.
Never cache private/authenticated data in shared caches.

situations.question: After a deploy, some users run new HTML that references old, now-404'd JS chunks and the app white-screens. What caused it and how do you prevent it?
situations.answer: This is an HTTP caching/invalidation mismatch. The HTML was cached (or served stale by a CDN) and still points at old hashed chunk filenames, while the new deploy replaced those chunks — so the referenced files 404 and the app fails to boot. Prevention: serve the HTML entry with Cache-Control: no-cache (always revalidate) so users always fetch HTML that references the current chunk hashes, while serving the hashed JS/CSS with long max-age + immutable. Keep the previous deploy's chunks available for a grace period (don't delete old hashed files immediately) so in-flight sessions don't break. Add a runtime guard: catch chunk-load errors and force a reload to pick up fresh HTML. And ensure CDN purge/versioning is part of the deploy so the edge doesn't serve stale HTML.

topics.title: Serverless & AWS Lambda
topics.summary: Run backend functions without managing servers — auto-scaling, pay-per-invocation event handlers. The frontend dev's gateway to backend logic.
topics.explanation: Serverless (FaaS — Functions as a Service) lets you deploy individual functions that the cloud runs on demand, scaling automatically and billing per invocation/duration rather than for always-on servers. AWS Lambda is the canonical example; equivalents include Cloudflare Workers, Vercel/Netlify Functions, and Google Cloud Functions. Why frontend engineers care: it's the easiest way to add a backend — for the API-key proxy pattern, webhooks receivers, BFF (backend-for-frontend) endpoints, image processing, auth callbacks, SSR/edge rendering, and scheduled jobs — without owning infrastructure. Mental model: a function has a HANDLER that receives an event (HTTP request via API Gateway, an S3 upload, a queue message, a cron trigger) and returns a response. Key concepts seniors must know: 1) COLD STARTS — the first invocation (or after idle) pays init latency while the runtime spins up; mitigate with smaller bundles, provisioned concurrency, lighter runtimes, or edge runtimes (Cloudflare Workers / Lambda@Edge) which have near-zero cold starts. 2) STATELESS & ephemeral — no reliable local disk or in-memory state between invocations; persist to a DB/cache; reuse connections across warm invocations (declare clients outside the handler). 3) Execution limits — max duration (e.g. 15 min), memory (which also scales CPU), and payload size; long jobs should be broken up or moved to queues/step functions. 4) Cost model — cheap at low/spiky traffic, but can be more expensive than a container at sustained high throughput; also watch the 'death by a thousand invocations'. 5) Concurrency & throttling — Lambda scales out per concurrent request; downstream DBs can get overwhelmed, so use connection pooling (RDS Proxy) or serverless-friendly data stores. 6) Cold security/ops — least-privilege IAM roles, secrets via env/secret manager, observability (structured logs, tracing). Edge functions are increasingly the sweet spot for frontend-adjacent needs (low latency, fast cold start) while full Lambda suits heavier compute.
topics.analogy: Serverless is renting a kitchen by the dish instead of owning a restaurant: functions spin up on demand, scale automatically, and bill per call — but the first order after a quiet spell waits for the kitchen to warm up (cold start).
keyPoints: Functions run on demand, auto-scale, and bill per invocation.
Great for API-key proxies, webhooks, BFF endpoints, and cron jobs.
Cold starts add first-hit latency (mitigate: small bundles, provisioned/edge).
Stateless and ephemeral — persist to a DB; reuse clients across warm calls.
Limits on duration/memory/payload; watch DB concurrency.

situations.question: Your serverless API feels snappy under load but users report the very first request after a quiet period is slow. What's happening and how do you address it?
situations.answer: That's a cold start: when no warm instance is available (first hit, or after the function's been idle and scaled to zero), the platform has to initialize a new execution environment — load the runtime, your bundle, and run init code — before handling the request. Subsequent requests reuse the warm instance and are fast. To address it: shrink the deployment bundle and dependencies, move heavy client/connection setup outside the handler so it's reused on warm invocations, and pick a lighter/faster-starting runtime. If the latency is user-facing and unacceptable, use provisioned concurrency (keep N instances warm) or move latency-sensitive endpoints to an edge runtime like Cloudflare Workers / Lambda@Edge, which have near-zero cold starts. I'd also question whether the workload is steady-and-high enough that a small always-on container would be both faster and cheaper than serverless.

topics.title: Frontend Testing Strategy
topics.summary: The testing pyramid for UIs: many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, a thin layer of E2E. Test behavior, not implementation.
topics.explanation: A senior is expected to define a testing STRATEGY, not just write a test. The framing is the testing pyramid (or 'trophy' for frontends): 1) UNIT tests — small, fast, isolated; pure functions, hooks, reducers, utilities. Use Jest/Vitest. Many of these. 2) INTEGRATION/COMPONENT tests — render a component (or a few together) and assert on behavior the way a user experiences it. React Testing Library (RTL) is the standard, and its core philosophy is key: query by accessible roles/text (what the user sees), interact via events, assert on output — DO NOT test implementation details (state internals, instance methods), because those tests break on refactors that don't change behavior. This layer gives the best confidence-per-effort for frontends (hence Kent Dodds' 'testing trophy' weights integration heaviest). 3) END-TO-END (E2E) — drive the real app in a real browser across the real stack. Cypress / Playwright. Slow and more brittle, so keep them few and focused on critical user journeys (login, checkout). 4) Supporting types — visual regression (Chromatic/Percy), accessibility tests (axe), contract tests, and performance budgets. Cross-cutting senior knowledge: mock at the boundary — intercept network with MSW (Mock Service Worker) rather than mocking fetch ad-hoc, so tests exercise real request/response handling; avoid over-mocking (mocked tests that pass while the app is broken). Aim for tests that give CONFIDENCE and survive refactors; coverage % is a weak proxy — a high number with implementation-detail tests is worse than fewer behavior tests. Also: flaky tests erode trust, so address async waits properly (await findBy*, not arbitrary sleeps), and run tests in CI on every PR.
topics.analogy: A good test suite is shaped like a trophy: lots of integration tests in the middle (best confidence per effort), fewer unit and E2E. Coverage % tells you what ran, not whether bugs would be caught.
keyPoints: Testing trophy: weight integration tests heaviest.
Test behavior, not implementation; query by role/text.
Mock at the network boundary with MSW.
Keep a thin layer of E2E for critical journeys.
Coverage ≠ confidence; fix flaky async waits (findBy over sleeps).

situations.question: Your team has 90% code coverage but bugs still reach production regularly. What's likely wrong with the test suite and how would you fix the strategy?
situations.answer: High coverage with low confidence usually means the tests assert implementation details rather than behavior — testing internal state, calling component methods, or heavily mocking everything so the tests pass even when the integrated app is broken. Coverage measures lines executed, not meaningful assertions. I'd shift the strategy toward the testing trophy: weight integration/component tests (React Testing Library) that render real components and assert what a user sees, mock only at the network boundary with MSW so request handling is actually exercised, and add a thin layer of Playwright E2E tests over the few critical journeys (login, checkout) that catch cross-stack breakage. I'd stop chasing the coverage number, delete brittle implementation-detail tests, fix flaky async waits (findBy over sleeps), and gate PRs on the behavior-focused suite. The goal is confidence-per-test, not a percentage.

topics.title: Integrating AI / LLMs into Frontends
topics.summary: Streaming responses, prompt design, token costs, the secret-key proxy, and UX patterns for non-deterministic, latent, sometimes-wrong AI.
topics.explanation: Adding AI (LLMs like Claude/GPT) to a product is increasingly a frontend responsibility, and it raises distinct engineering and UX challenges. Architecture: 1) NEVER call the model provider directly from the client — the API key is secret, so route through your backend/serverless proxy (see API Keys & Lambda topics), which also lets you add auth, rate limiting, logging, and prompt-injection guards. 2) STREAMING — LLMs generate token-by-token and full responses can take many seconds; stream tokens to the UI (Server-Sent Events or fetch streaming / ReadableStream) so users see output immediately. This is the single biggest UX win. 3) Latency & loading UX — show partial output, typing indicators, and allow CANCELLATION (AbortController) since users change their minds; debounce expensive calls. 4) Cost & tokens — you pay per input+output token, so be deliberate about context size (don't stuff entire histories), cache where possible, and consider smaller/cheaper models for simple tasks. 5) Non-determinism & errors — the same prompt can give different answers and models hallucinate; design for it: constrain with structured outputs/JSON schema/tool-use, validate responses before acting on them, show sources, and never auto-execute destructive actions from raw model output without confirmation. 6) Prompt design lives partly in the frontend/BFF — system prompts, few-shot examples, and assembling context (RAG: retrieve relevant docs and inject them). 7) Safety/UX — handle refusals gracefully, set expectations ('AI can make mistakes'), provide feedback controls, and respect privacy (what user data goes into prompts). 8) Tool/function calling — let the model request actions your frontend/backend executes, enabling agents. Senior signal: treating AI as an unreliable, latent, costly dependency that needs streaming, validation, guardrails, and good fallbacks — not as a magic deterministic API.
topics.analogy: Adding an LLM is integrating an unreliable, slow, costly oracle: route it through your backend (keep the key secret), stream tokens so it feels fast, and validate/guard its output because it can be wrong.
keyPoints: Never call the model provider from the client — proxy via your backend.
Stream tokens (SSE/ReadableStream) — the single biggest UX win.
Allow cancellation (AbortController) and show partial output.
Treat output as non-deterministic: validate, cite, never auto-run destructive actions.
Control cost/latency: tight context (RAG), caching, smaller models for simple tasks.

situations.question: You're adding an AI assistant to the app. The PM wants it to feel fast and trustworthy. What are the key engineering and UX decisions you'd make?
situations.answer: Architecture first: calls go through our backend/serverless proxy, never the client, so the provider key stays secret and we can add auth, rate limiting, logging, and input/output guards. For 'fast': stream tokens to the UI (SSE or fetch ReadableStream) so users see output within ~100ms instead of waiting for the whole response, show a typing indicator, and provide a Stop button (AbortController) plus retry. For 'trustworthy': treat the model as non-deterministic and fallible — constrain it with structured/JSON outputs and validate them before acting, show sources/citations for factual claims, never auto-execute destructive actions without explicit confirmation, and set honest expectations ('AI can make mistakes') with a feedback control. I'd manage cost/latency by keeping context tight (RAG to inject only relevant docs rather than dumping history), caching where possible, and using a smaller model for simple tasks. And I'd handle refusals/errors gracefully with clear fallbacks. Net: stream for speed, validate and cite for trust, proxy and guard for safety.

topics.title: Design Patterns in Frontend
topics.summary: Observer, Strategy, Factory, Compound Components, Render Props, and HOCs — when to reach for each in a modern React codebase.
topics.explanation: Design patterns are reusable solutions to recurring design problems. Senior engineers apply them intentionally and can justify the choice. Frontend-relevant patterns: 1) Observer — a subject notifies many subscribers of state changes. The foundation of event systems, RxJS, and Redux. Directly used when building event emitters, publish/subscribe buses for cross-component communication, and WebSocket message dispatching. 2) Strategy — swap an algorithm at runtime behind a common interface. Useful for validation rules, sorting/filtering logic, or rendering different chart types behind one component. 3) Factory — create objects without specifying exact class. In React: a function that returns a component variant based on config. Useful for dynamic form fields, pluggable renderers, icon registries. 4) Singleton — one global instance. Redux store, React context values, service clients. Overused; be deliberate. 5) Compound Components — expose a set of related components (like <Select> + <Option>) that share state through context. Gives consumers flexible composition without prop drilling. 6) Render Props / Children as Function — pass render logic as a prop/children for inversion of control. Largely replaced by hooks but still found in lib APIs. 7) HOC (Higher-Order Component) — a function that takes a component and returns an enhanced one. Classic pattern for cross-cutting concerns (auth guards, analytics, theming). Mostly replaced by hooks + composition in modern React. 8) Provider pattern — supply data/services to a tree via context, avoiding prop-drilling. The senior skill: recognize which pattern solves the actual problem with minimal added complexity, and prefer composition over inheritance and hooks over HOCs in modern React.
topics.analogy: Design patterns are named recipes for recurring problems: Observer (a newsletter), Strategy (swappable drill bits), Compound Components (a LEGO set). The skill is picking the one that fits with the least added complexity.
keyPoints: Observer/Pub-Sub backs event systems, Redux, RxJS.
Strategy swaps algorithms via a map instead of if/switch.
Compound Components share state across related parts via Context.
HOCs/Render Props are mostly replaced by hooks in modern React.
Prefer composition over inheritance; hooks over HOCs.

situations.question: You're building a data table that needs to support many different cell types — text, badge, date, currency, custom action buttons. How do you architect the cell rendering?
situations.answer: This is a Strategy / Factory pattern problem. I'd define a column config that maps a column type to a renderer function or component: a cellRenderers map (text, badge, date, currency, action) where each renderer receives the cell value and row data. The table iterates columns, looks up the renderer by type, and calls it — so adding a new cell type is just adding a new entry to the map, with no changes to the table component itself. For one-off custom renderers, columns can accept a renderCell function directly (escape hatch). This is the approach used by AG Grid, TanStack Table, etc. I'd avoid a large if/switch tree in the table component — that's the pattern that forces you to modify the core every time the product adds a new column type.

topics.title: Advanced TypeScript
topics.summary: Generics, conditional types, mapped types, utility types, discriminated unions — writing type-safe, reusable, maintainable TypeScript.
topics.explanation: Senior engineers use TypeScript's type system as a tool for correctness and expressibility, not just a linter. Key advanced concepts: 1) Generics — parameterize types to be reusable across value types; a generic function (function identity<T>(x: T): T) maintains the relationship between input and output types. 2) Conditional types (T extends U ? A : B) — compute types based on type relationships; basis for inference with infer keyword. 3) Mapped types — transform every property in a type (e.g. { [K in keyof T]: ... }); basis for all the built-in utility types. 4) Template literal types — build string union types from other unions ('get' | 'post' combined with paths → full URL types). 5) Discriminated unions — a union of object types with a common literal tag field; TypeScript narrows the type in a switch/if; the idiomatic pattern for state machines and action types (Redux, useReducer). 6) Built-in utility types seniors should know cold: Partial<T>, Required<T>, Readonly<T>, Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K>, Record<K,V>, Extract<T,U>, Exclude<T,U>, ReturnType<F>, Parameters<F>, Awaited<T>, NonNullable<T>. 7) satisfies operator — validate a value against a type while inferring the narrowest type. 8) Type predicates (is keyword) and assertion functions for user-defined type guards. Key philosophy: use the type system to make illegal states unrepresentable; prefer discriminated unions over boolean flags and nullable fields to model state cleanly.
topics.analogy: Advanced TypeScript is using the type system to make illegal states impossible: discriminated unions instead of loose booleans, generics for reuse, and mapped/conditional types to compute types from types.
keyPoints: Generics + constraints build reusable, type-safe APIs.
Discriminated unions model state cleanly (vs multiple booleans).
Conditional + mapped types power the built-in utilities.
Know the utility types cold (Pick/Omit/Record/ReturnType/Awaited).
satisfies validates without widening; make illegal states unrepresentable.

situations.question: A teammate models a user's fetch state with three separate booleans: isLoading, isError, isSuccess. What's wrong with this and how would you fix it?
situations.answer: Three independent booleans create 8 combinations, but only 4 are valid (idle, loading, error, success). You get impossible states like isLoading && isSuccess === true, and conditional rendering becomes a chain of fragile if-else checks that future engineers will inevitably get wrong. The fix is a discriminated union: one status field with a literal type ('idle' | 'loading' | 'error' | 'success'), where the error and data fields only exist on the relevant variant. TypeScript then narrows correctly in switch statements — you can't access state.data unless status === 'success', and you can't access state.error unless status === 'error'. This is the core principle: make illegal states unrepresentable by the type system.

topics.title: GraphQL vs REST
topics.summary: Choosing between REST and GraphQL — query flexibility, over/under-fetching, N+1, schema-first development, and when each wins.
